summary: 13th-century Scottish/British Scholastic philosopher known as Doctor Subtilis, author of the Ordinatio. Famous for the concepts of haecceity ('thisness') and the univocity of being, his defense of the Immaculate Conception, and his influence on Gerard Manley Hopkins's ideas of inscape and instress.

works.name: Haecceity ('Thisness')
works.description: Haecceity is the concept coined by Scotus's followers (and developed from his philosophy) denoting the irreducible properties of a thing that make it a particular individual rather than a member of a class. Often translated as 'thisness,' it contrasts with Aquinas's quiddity ('whatness'). C.S. Peirce later used the term, and The Social Construction of Reality by Berger and Luckmann borrows it.

clues.clue: Coined haecceity ('thisness') - the individuating properties that make something a particular thing
examples: This philosopher's followers coined a term for the particular traits that individuate something, its haecceity
developed the concept of haecceitas to denote the distinct qualities, or 'thisness,' of a thing

clues.clue: C.S. Peirce used haecceity to outline 'secondness' - how we perceive events as separate
examples: C.S. Pierce used this term to outline how we perceive events as separate from other events and experience 'secondness.'

clues.clue: The Social Construction of Reality (Berger and Luckmann) borrows haecceity from the Ordinatio
examples: The Social Construction of Reality borrows the term 'haecceity,' referring to the qualities that define a certain entity's specific identity, from the Ordinatio of this philosopher

clues.clue: William of Alnwick (his student) rejected distinction between haecceity and nature
examples: William of Alnwick diverged from this concept's originator, his teacher Duns Scotus, by rejecting a distinction between this concept and nature.

works.name: Univocity of Being
works.description: Scotus's doctrine that properties like 'goodness' mean the same thing when applied to God and to creatures - the difference is one of degree, not meaning. This directly opposes Aquinas's analogical predication. Deleuze wrote of an 'ontological' version in his commentary on Spinoza. The Radical Orthodox School (Catherine Pickstock, John Milbank) blames Scotus for ushering in modernity through this concept.

clues.clue: Univocity of being: properties of God mean the same as when applied to humans, differing only in degree
examples: contended that words mean the same thing whether they are applied to God or to things, which is his concept of 'univocity of being'
the idea that the properties of God, such as being good, mean the same thing when they are applied to people or objects

clues.clue: Contrasts with Aquinas's doctrine that God's goodness is only analogous to human goodness
examples: This philosopher held that God's goodness is only analogous to human goodness, in contrast to the idea of univocity of being.

clues.clue: Radical Orthodox School (Catherine Pickstock, John Milbank) blames Scotus for ushering in modernity
examples: Catherine Pickstock and John Milbank are among a set of Cambridge thinkers whose animating philosophy is that this thinker can be blamed for ushering in modernity

clues.clue: Deleuze wrote of an 'ontological' version of univocity in commentary on Spinoza
examples: Deleuze wrote of an 'ontological' version of this concept as part of a commentary on Spinoza.

works.name: Ordinatio and Scholastic Works
works.description: The Ordinatio is Scotus's major work, compiled from his Oxford lectures. It serves as a commentary on Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences and introduces both haecceity and univocity of being. Scotus also wrote a Treatise on God as the First Principle and commentaries on Aristotle's categories and Porphyry's Isagoge.

clues.clue: Ordinatio: compiled from Oxford lectures, a commentary on Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences
examples: this medieval philosopher, who compiled his lectures at Oxford into his Ordinatio
This author of the Ordinatio, who was often called Doctor Subtilis

clues.clue: Wrote a Treatise on God as the First Principle
examples: This author of a Treatise on God as the First Principle devised a logical concept of 'thisness' called haeccitas.

clues.clue: Wrote a commentary on Aristotle's categories; rejected existence-essence distinction posited by Aquinas
examples: This man rejected the existence-essence distinction posited by Aquinas, and his earliest works were commentaries entitled the 'parva logicalia.'
This medieval philosopher wrote a famous commentary on Aristotle's categories.

clues.clue: Developed an argument for God's existence based on first efficient cause
examples: This teacher of Francis of Mayrone developed an extremely convoluted argument for the existence of God based on a first efficient cause.
Scotus put forth a metaphysical argument based on efficient causes

works.name: Gerard Manley Hopkins and Inscape
works.description: The Victorian poet and Jesuit priest Gerard Manley Hopkins drew on Scotus's concept of haecceity when formulating his ideas of inscape and instress. Hopkins wrote the poem 'Duns Scotus's Oxford,' which calls Scotus 'he who of all men most sways my spirits to peace' and concludes with 'he fired France for Mary without spot' (a reference to Scotus's defense of the Immaculate Conception).

clues.clue: Hopkins drew on haecceity to formulate his ideas of inscape and instress
examples: Hopkins drew on this scholastic thinker's concept of haecceity when formulating his ideas of inscape and instress.
Reading this philosopher inspired a certain poet's fluid use of a term that generally corresponds to the idea that all creatures in the universe dynamically 'self'

examples: this poem that describes a certain 'Towery city' as '[c]uckoo-echoing, bell-swarmed, lark charmed, rook-racked, river-rounded.'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Known as Doctor Subtilis for his penetrating thought. British/Scottish philosopher whose place of birth became a word for stupidity ('dunce') that names a conical cap. Defended the Immaculate Conception six centuries before the Church made it doctrine. Teacher of Francis of Mayrone. Opposed by the Ockhamists; his realism about universals was countered by Ockham's nominalism.

clues.clue: Known as Doctor Subtilis
examples: This thinker was often known as 'Doctor Subtilis.'
given the accolade Doctor Subtilis

clues.clue: British place of birth became word for stupidity ('dunce'); names a conical cap
examples: His British place of birth became a word for stupidity that names a conical cap.

clues.clue: Defended the Immaculate Conception six centuries before the Church made it doctrine
examples: He defended the Immaculate Conception six centuries before the Church made it doctrine.
This philosopher theorized that Christ's body did not corrupt because every person of the Trinity is formally distinct.

clues.clue: Opposed by Ockhamists; Scotists vs. Ockhamists on nominalism vs. realism about universals
examples: The Scotists were largely opposed by a school of theologians named for this philosopher
In contrast to Duns Scotus's realism, this thinker's Summa Logicae solved the problem of universals

clues.clue: Attacked divine illumination theory of Henry of Ghent in his Ordinatio
examples: This philosopher attacked Henry of Ghent's illuminationism in his Ordinatio.

clues.clue: Argued that angels have individual, incomplete knowledge as a sign of their haecceity (contra Aquinas who said angels know everything)
examples: This philosopher argued that Aquinas's claim that angels already know everything about the universe would mean that they would have nothing to talk about with each other

clues.clue: One of the last major figures to write a commentary on Boethius's version of the Isagoge
examples: One of the last major figures to write a commentary on Boethius' version of the Isagoge was this British philosopher

clues.clue: Heidegger thought he was writing his Habilitation on Scotus, but the text De modis significandi was actually by Thomas of Erfurt
examples: Martin Heidegger thought he was writing his Habilitation thesis on this man, but the second part is on the logical treatise De modis significandi, attributed to this man but actually by Thomas of Erfurt.

comprehensive_summary:
John Duns Scotus is a 13th-century British/Scottish Scholastic philosopher known as Doctor Subtilis ('the Subtle Doctor'). He is primarily associated with two philosophical concepts: haecceity and the univocity of being.

Haecceity (from the Latin for 'thisness') denotes the irreducible properties that make a thing a particular individual rather than merely a member of a class. The term was coined by Scotus's followers based on his philosophy and contrasts with Aquinas's concept of quiddity ('whatness'). C.S. Peirce later used the term to outline how we perceive 'secondness,' and Berger and Luckmann's The Social Construction of Reality borrows it from the Ordinatio.

The univocity of being holds that properties like 'goodness' mean the same thing when applied to God as to creatures - the ultimate difference is one of degree, not of meaning. This directly opposes Thomas Aquinas's analogical predication. The Radical Orthodox School (Catherine Pickstock, John Milbank) blames Scotus for ushering in modernity through this concept, and Gilles Deleuze wrote of an 'ontological' version in his commentary on Spinoza.

Scotus's major work, the Ordinatio, was compiled from his Oxford lectures and serves as a commentary on Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences. He also wrote A Treatise on God as the First Principle and developed an argument for God's existence based on first efficient cause. He rejected the existence-essence distinction posited by Aquinas and attacked Henry of Ghent's divine illumination theory.

The Victorian poet Gerard Manley Hopkins drew heavily on Scotus's haecceity when formulating his ideas of inscape and instress. Hopkins's poem 'Duns Scotus's Oxford' describes the city as a 'Towery city' that is 'cuckoo-echoing, bell-swarmed, lark charmed, rook-racked, river-rounded,' calls Scotus 'he who of all men most sways my spirits to peace,' and concludes 'he fired France for Mary without spot,' referencing Scotus's famous defense of the Immaculate Conception, which he championed six centuries before the Church made it official doctrine.

Scotus's British place of birth gave rise to the word 'dunce,' naming a conical cap. His Scotist school opposed the Ockhamists on the problem of universals. Martin Heidegger wrote his Habilitation thesis thinking it was about Scotus, but the text De modis significandi was actually by Thomas of Erfurt. Scotus argued that angels have individual, incomplete knowledge as a sign of their haecceity, contra Aquinas who claimed angels already know everything.

Work: compiled from Oxford lectures, this commentary on Peter Lombard's Four Books of Sentences introduces both haecceity and univocity of being
Philosopher: Gerard Manley Hopkins drew on this thinker's concept of haecceity to formulate his ideas of inscape and instress
Philosopher: a poem about this thinker's 'Oxford' describes the city as 'Towery' and 'cuckoo-echoing, bell-swarmed' and concludes 'he fired France for Mary without spot'
Philosopher: defended the Immaculate Conception six centuries before the Church made it doctrine; theorized Christ's body did not corrupt because every person of the Trinity is formally distinct
